issue: Strengthening the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons review process

entries.position_summary: The United States supports the establishment of a working group to improve the NPT review process, identify best practices, and structure discussions to foster constructive engagement. It sees the group as a means to reduce substantive differences among parties.
entries.quote: 2. The United States welcomed the establishment of the working group as an opportunity to consider practical ways to improve the existing review process and to identify and reinforce best practices from recent experience. This working group can still help to structure the review process in ways that foster constructive engagement to reduce substantive differences.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports strengthening the NPT review process, submitting working papers with options and updates aligned with the Chair’s draft recommendations and a late compromise proposal. It views these proposals as enjoying broad support and forming a solid basis for consensus on review-process reforms.
entries.quote: 1. The United States submitted a working paper1 to the meeting of the working group on further strengthening the review process of the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons, held on 24 to 28 July 2023, which identified several areas in which the review process could be improved and laying out options and issues for consideration by States parties to the Non-Proliferation Treaty. The present working paper provides an update based on the draft recommendations of the Chair (NPT/CONF.2026/WG.I/CRP.2/Rev.1), which the Chair later submitted as a working paper to the first session (held in 2023) of the Preparatory Committee for the 2026 Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (NPT/CONF.2026/PC.I/WP.34) and on the deliberations of the working group, including the compromise proposal submitted by four States at the end of the working group’s deliberations. Collectively, these proposals had broad support and provide a solid basis for reaching consensus on measures to strengthen the Non-Proliferation Treaty review process.

entries.position_summary: The country urges building consensus in the NPT Preparatory Committee to lay the groundwork for a successful Review Conference next year. It supports strengthening the NPT review process and achieving positive outcomes.
entries.quote: To conclude, I wish this preparatory committee build further consensus, and lay a good foundation for achieving positive results at the review conference next year.

entries.position_summary: China urges States Parties to learn from past failed review cycles, build on the 11th RevCon PrepCom work, adopt a pragmatic approach, and enhance dialogue to reach consensus on key elements for inclusion in the 2026 final document.
entries.quote: In light of the above, China suggests that NPT States Parties fully draw lessons from past two consecutive NPT review cycles ending without a consensual outcome, build on the work of the first and second PrepCom for the 11th NPT Review conference, take a rational and pragmatic attitude, strengthen dialogue and consultation, seek consensus on the following elements and include them in the final document of the 11th Review Conference in 2026.

entries.position_summary: The country argues that duplication across cluster groups and Main Committees undermines the NPT review process’s effectiveness and efficiency, leading to repetitive statements and less interactive deliberation. It advocates reforms to improve transparency, accountability, coordination, and continuity, and to better address cross-cutting linkages among the Treaty’s three pillars.
entries.quote: 4. The mandate for the working group was “to discuss and make recommendations to the Preparatory Committee on measures that would improve the effectiveness, efficiency, transparency, accountability, coordination and continuity of the review process of the Treaty”. The duplication in coverage of substantive issues across the three cluster groups of the Preparatory Committee meetings and the three Main Committees of the Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons, and more so between the Main Committees and their subsidiary bodies, impedes the effectiveness and efficiency of the review process. This leads to repetition of well-known talking points and takes time away from interactive deliberations among States parties. While there are linkages among the three pillars that these clusters and the Main Committees represent, and those pillars are in many ways mutually reinforcing, the current structure has not proven effective at elucidating those connections or at addressing other cross-cutting issues.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates reforming the NPT review process by replacing the current pillar-by-pillar approach in the three Main Committees with an article-by-article review conducted in plenary sessions.
entries.quote: • Alternatively, replacing the pillar-by-pillar review in the three Main Committees with an article-by-article review in plenary sessions.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates clarifying the division of labor between Main Committees and subsidiary bodies in the NPT review process. It proposes that Main Committees focus on reviewing past actions while subsidiary bodies craft future recommendations, or alternatively, that issues be divided substantively with flexibility to adjust over time.
entries.quote: • Defining a clear division between the Main Committees and the subsidiary bodies. One option could be that each of the Main Committees focuses on the review of past actions, while their subsidiary bodies focus on recommendations for future actions. Another option would be to divide issues along substantive lines, in which case it could be useful to preserve the flexibility to adjust those divisions from time to time.

entries.position_summary: The country proposes refocusing the Preparatory Committee on compiling and exchanging views and then assigning proposals to Main Committees or subsidiary bodies for structured discussion, rather than forcing end-stage compromises. This aims to deepen understanding and produce more focused, relevant Review Conferences.
entries.quote: 12. An alternative would be for the Preparatory Committee to focus on compiling and exchanging views and subsequently assigning proposals for structured discussion to one of the Main Committees or subsidiary bodies. Rather than seeking to prejudge the outcome of the Review Conference by resolving differences, the Preparatory Committee would allow for a more in-depth exchange of views to increase understanding. This could make better use of the preparatory committees and lead to more focused and topically relevant review conferences while avoiding the displeasure that has sometimes resulted from efforts to force a compromise at the end of the last Preparatory Committee meeting.

entries.position_summary: Supports early appointment of Preparatory Committee leadership and the Review Conference President-Elect at least a year in advance to improve consultations and preparation for those meetings.
entries.quote: • Early naming of the leadership of each Preparatory Committee and the President-Elect of the Review Conference a year ahead of time maximized the ability of each to carry out such consultations and otherwise prepare to lead those meetings.

entries.position_summary: Supports formalizing an NPT Bureau by having the three PrepCom Chairs and the Review Conference President-elect operate as a coordinated team, with UNODA providing administrative and logistical support funded through assessed contributions. This aims to improve the efficiency and coherence of the NPT review process.
entries.quote: • Formalizing the Non-Proliferation Treaty Bureau by encouraging the three Preparatory Committee Chairs and the President-Elect of the Review Conference to work together as a team. The Office for Disarmament Affairs could provide administrative and logistical support (subject to the usual assessed funding by States parties).

entries.position_summary: Supports formalizing good practices to strengthen the NPT review process, including early appointment of PrepCom/subsidiary body Chairs and the Review Conference President, establishing a President’s bureau, conducting regional consultations, and formalizing supportive roles for UNODA and the IAEA to improve effectiveness, coordination, and continuity.
entries.quote: 15. The working group had extensive discussions on proposals to formalize these good practices, many of which garnered general support. Paragraphs 10 and 12 of the Chair’s working paper call for early appointment of the Preparatory Committee and subsidiary body Chairs and of the President of the Review Conference, and the establishment of the “President’s bureau” to coordinate consultations and preparations for the Review Conference. Paragraph 21 recommends that they undertake consultations with States parties on a regional basis. Paragraph 15 recommends formalizing the roles of the Office for Disarmament Affairs and the International Atomic Energy Agency in supporting the review process. Together, these recommendations offer concrete steps to improve the effectiveness and efficiency of the review process and to improve coordination and continuity between the meetings of the review cycle.

entries.position_summary: China supports interactive, inclusive discussions to strengthen the NPT review process, emphasizing that exchanging diverse views helps assess the rationality and feasibility of proposals. It views such dialogue as positively contributing to further study and efforts by States Parties to improve the review process.
entries.quote: Remarks by H.E. Ambassador LI Song on Strengthening the NPT Review Process at the First Meeting of the Preparatory Committee for the 2026 NPT Review Conference August 9, 2023, Vienna International Center Mr. Chair, At the meeting of the Working Group on further strengthening the review process of the NPT, which was held two weeks ago, many views and proposals were put forward by delegations, and many new and different ideas emerged in the discussions. Such interactive discussions will help all parties understand further different views and positions and explore the rationality and feasibility of relevant ideas. This is of positive significance for NPT States parties to further study and discuss the strengthening of the review process of the Treaty.

entries.position_summary: China raises concerns about AUKUS naval nuclear propulsion and calls for the NPT review process and the IAEA to conduct thorough, transparent, and inclusive discussions on the safeguards challenges it poses, with decisions made by consensus.
entries.quote: Since 2023, the Permanent Mission of China in Vienna convened three workshops to thoroughly discuss the legal, technical and procedural aspects of AUKUS nuclear submarine cooperation. The NPT review process and the IAEA should continue in-depth discussions about the safeguards issues and its challenges involved in the AUKUS nuclear submarines cooperation through a transparent, open, inclusive and sustainable intergovernmental process and make decisions by consensus.

entries.position_summary: China, via its Arms Control and Disarmament Association, congratulates and expresses firm support for the new round of the NPT review process. The statement signals endorsement of and engagement with the PrepCom and the 2026 Review Conference process.
entries.quote: StatementbyMr.Li Chijiang, Secretary-General oftheChinaArms Controland DisarmamentAssociation, at thePreparatory Committeefor the2026Review Conference oftheParties to theTreaty ontheNon-Proliferation ofNuclearWeapons (Vienna, August 2023) Distinguished Chairperson andrepresentatives, First of all, on behalf of the ChinaArms Control and DisarmamentAssociation, I would liketo express our congratulations and firm support to the new round of review process oftheTreaty ontheNon-Proliferation ofNuclearWeapons.

entries.position_summary: China urges urgent progress on negative security assurances, asserting that non-nuclear-weapon States are entitled to be free from nuclear threats and that NSAs are a low-hanging fruit for disarmament. It wants NSAs to be a key outcome of this review cycle and notes it has submitted a working paper and stands ready to elaborate its proposals.
entries.quote: Statement by the Chinese Delegation on the Issue of Negative Security Assurances at the Third Session of the Preparatory Committee for the 2026 NPT Review Conference Mr. President, Non-nuclear-weapon States have assumed the obligation not to develop or acquire nuclear weapons, so they are entitled to the right of being free from the threat of nuclear weapons. It is urgent for address the issue of negative security assurances. It serves the security interests and meets the general expectations of non -nuclear-weapon States. It is a “low-hanging fruit” in the field of nuclear disarmament and should be an important outcome of this review process. The Chinese delegation has submitted a working paper on the issue of negative security assurances to the second preparatory committee for the eleventh review conference of the Treaty, and is willing to further elaborate on the following propositions.

entries.position_summary: Advocates streamlining the NPT review structure by revising Main Committee mandates to avoid duplication, assigning Articles I–III to Main Committee II, Article VI to Main Committee I, and Article IV to Main Committee III.
entries.quote: • Revising the mandates of the Main Committees and clusters to eliminate duplication. For example, articles I, II and III, the central non-proliferation provisions of the Treaty, could be remanded to Main Committee II, while Main Committee I could focus on article VI and Main Committee III on article IV.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports recommendations to enhance the NPT review process by dedicating a plenary session to inclusivity and urges further consideration and adoption of these proposals.
entries.quote: NPT/CONF.2026/PC.II/WP.4 recommends allocating a plenary session of the Review Conference to discussion of inclusivity. The United States welcomes and encourages further consideration and adoption of these recommendations.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports using the NPT Preparatory Committee to identify areas of convergence and recommend focused discussions. It endorses the concrete recommendations in paragraphs 11 and 13 of the Chair’s working paper.
entries.quote: 13. The working group gave serious consideration to using the Preparatory Committee to identify areas of convergence and recommend areas of focused discussion. Paragraphs 11 and 13 of the Chair’s working paper contain concrete recommendations in this regard, which the United States would support.

entries.position_summary: The country urges stricter controls on nuclear-related exports and cooperation to prevent any assistance to nuclear weapons R&D. It voices concern that AUKUS nuclear-powered submarine cooperation could harm international peace and security and strain the IAEA safeguards system, and supports addressing related safeguards questions through a transparent, inclusive process under the NPT review and IAEA frameworks with consensus-based decisions. It warns that such cooperation may undermine regional strategic balance and stability.
entries.quote: Take practical measures to ensure that nuclear-related exports and cooperation are fully in line with the object and purpose of the NPT and will not directly or indirectly assist in the research and development of nuclear weapons or other nuclear explosive devices. Pay attention to the impact of AUKUS nuclear-powered submarine cooperation on international peace and security and the IAEA safeguards system. Support discussing the safeguards issues involved in the above-mentioned cooperation through a transparent, open and inclusive intergovernmental process under the NPT review process and the IAEA framework, and make decision by consensus. Meanwhile, be vigilant that such cooperation undermines regional strategic balance and stability and intensifies regional tensions.

entries.position_summary: The United States submits a working paper advocating practical steps to strengthen the NPT review process and reinforce best practices, building on the working group's efforts. It supports improving the review process ahead of the 2026 Review Conference.
entries.quote: NPT /CONF.2026/PC.II/WP.4 Preparatory Committee for the 2026 Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the 22 May 2024 Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons Original: English Second session Geneva, 22 July–2 August 2024 Strengthening the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons review process: practical steps to improve the process and reinforce best practices Update: building on the work of the working group Working paper submitted by the United States of America

entries.position_summary: Supports allowing any State party to submit national reports and advocates for earlier submission deadlines so others can review them ahead of discussion. Emphasizes improving the procedural effectiveness of the NPT review process.
entries.quote: • The opportunity to present national reports should be open to all States parties that wish to do so. The deadline for submission of reports should be early enough that other States parties have sufficient time to review them before the discussion session.

entries.position_summary: The United States supports institutionalizing the constructive innovations from the 2015 review cycle to make the NPT review process more inclusive, effective, and efficient. It urges the working group and Preparatory Committee to endorse and regularize these good practices and signals its readiness to support them.
entries.quote: 14. The 2015 review cycle resulted in several constructive innovations that helped to make the process more inclusive, effective and efficient and also improved coordination and continuity within the leadership team. The working group should consider recommending that the Preparatory Committee endorse these good practices to reinforce and regularize their use. Among those new practices, the United States would be ready to endorse: 4/5 24-09063

entries.position_summary: Supports holding regional and topical workshops ahead of NPT meetings, organized by PrepCom and Review Conference leadership and interested States, funded voluntarily. Proposes establishing a mechanism to fund travel for representatives from smaller and less developed countries to enhance inclusive participation.
entries.quote: • Holding regional and topical workshops during the year ahead of each meeting. Some of these were organized by the Chair of the Preparatory Committee and the President-Elect of the Review Conference, and some by interested States parties. All of them were funded through voluntary funding from interested States parties. A mechanism for funding travel to such events by representatives of smaller, less developed countries could also be considered.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates stronger international cooperation on peaceful nuclear energy, highlighting the central role of the IAEA and calling on advanced states to support capacity building, including initiatives like 'Atoms for the Global South.' It also hopes the current NPT review cycle will generate new, creative measures to advance the treaty’s third pillar.
entries.quote: On peaceful uses of nuclear energy. With the emergence of new nuclear energy technologies and with growing number of countries operating or planning to operate nuclear power plants, international cooperation on peaceful uses should be further strengthened. It’s essential to give full play to the important role of the IAEA in this respect. States with advanced capabilities need to help capacity building of countries that want to develop peaceful nuclear energy. “Atoms for the Global South” deserves increased support. We hope this review cycle will come up with some new and creative ideas in promoting the third pillar of the NPT.

entries.position_summary: The paragraph supports a forward- and backward-looking NPT review process that evaluates implementation and identifies paths for further progress. It also emphasizes strengthening implementation of the Treaty and achieving its universality.
entries.quote: NPT/CONF.2026/PC.II/WP.4 Proliferation Treaty Review and Extension Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons decided that “Review Conferences should look forward as well as back. They should evaluate the results of the period they are reviewing, including the implementation of undertakings of the States parties under the Treaty, and identify the areas in which, and the means through which, further progress should be sought in the future. Review Conferences should also address specifically what might be done to strengthen the implementation of the Treaty and to achieve its universality”.

entries.position_summary: The statement urges nuclear-weapon States to fully implement Article VI, adhere to disarmament commitments agreed at past NPT Review Conferences, and publicly declare they do not intend to retain nuclear weapons permanently.
entries.quote: 3. Nuclear-weapon States should commit themselves to the effective implementation of Article 6 of the NPT, abide by consensuses on nuclear disarmament reached in previous NPT Review Conferences, and openly declare that they have no intention to seek permanent possession of nuclear weapons.

entries.position_summary: The country supports preserving and expanding attention to cross-cutting issues—transparency and reporting, education and capacity-building, and gender and diversity—within the NPT context. It proposes dedicating plenary sessions at Review Conferences and the Preparatory Committee to address these topics.
entries.quote: • Preserving and potentially expanding opportunities to address cross-cutting issues, such as transparency, reporting, education and capacity-building, gender and diversity. These could be addressed in dedicated plenary sessions of review conferences and the Preparatory Committee.

entries.position_summary: Supports streamlining the review process by allocating issues among Main Committees to reduce duplication and recommends that the third PrepCom establish subsidiary bodies for the Review Conference and set their agendas.
entries.quote: Paragraphs 5 to 7 of the Chair’s working paper present an allocation of issues among the Main Committees that greatly reduces duplication. Paragraph 10 recommends that the third session of the Preparatory Committee establish the subsidiary bodies for the Review Conference and decide the topics that they should discuss. Paragraph 26 2/5 24-09063

entries.position_summary: The country notes that despite broad convergence on risk reduction and on linking peaceful uses to the Sustainable Development Goals, the NPT Preparatory Committee has failed to reach consensus on substantive recommendations. This expresses concern about the effectiveness of the review process and implies a need to improve it.
entries.quote: Even when there is a strong convergence of interest on certain topics, such as risk reduction or linking peaceful uses to Sustainable Development Goals, the Preparatory Committee has never been able to reach consensus on substantive recommendations on those or any other topics.

entries.position_summary: Supports improving the NPT review process by reducing repetitive debates and endorsing measures to streamline discussions.
entries.quote: 6. The working group recognized the inefficiency of having repetitive debates and expressed broad support for actions to improve efficiency by reducing repetition.

entries.position_summary: The United States highlights its consistent national reporting on NPT implementation using the NWS reporting format and notes the growing practice among States parties. It supports dedicating time at formal PrepCom and Review Conference meetings for presentation and interactive discussion of national reports to enhance transparency and accountability.
entries.quote: 8. The United States has reported consistently on its own implementation of the Non-Proliferation Treaty and actions to strengthen the Treaty regime across all three pillars, including to the 2014 Preparatory Conference, the 2015 Review Conference and, most recently, the 2022 Review Conference (NPT/CONF.2020/57), using a reporting format adopted by the nuclear-weapon States. The practice of submitting national reports has grown steadily and is now well established. In 2015, 22 States parties submitted national reports, and in 2022, 40 States parties did so. These reports have usually been presented in side events, as there has not been place for such presentations at formal meetings of the Preparatory Committee or the Review Conference. The United States would support dedicating time at those formal meetings for presentation and interactive discussion of national reports. Such discussions would provide an enhanced mechanism for transparency and accountability.

entries.position_summary: The United States urges the Preparatory Committee to adopt a package of measures to improve the NPT review process, building on the Chair’s working paper. It also supports advancing the near-adopted proposal on transparency and accountability to consolidate emerging consensus from the working group.
entries.quote: 16. The United States looks forward to discussion of each of these issues, as well as proposals put forward by other States parties, at the upcoming Preparatory Committee meeting. This meeting offers an opportunity for States parties to consolidate the consensus that was nearly achieved in the working group by adopting a package of measures to improve the Non-Proliferation Treaty review process based on the Chair’s working paper and the alternative proposal on transparency and accountability that was nearly adopted at the end of the working group meeting. 24-09063 5/5

entries.position_summary: China highlights its contributions via working papers promoting nuclear risk reduction, addressing AUKUS nuclear submarine cooperation, and discussing Japan’s Fukushima water disposal as NPT-relevant safety concerns. It also advocates strengthening the NPT review process and proposes amendments to the Chair’s paper to guide future discussions.
entries.quote: For example, during this Preparatory Committee session, we submitted three working papers. Among them, the working paper on nuclear risk reduction has been highly valued and commended by some delegations, and the other two working papers are on the AUKUS nuclear submarine cooperation and the disposal of nuclear contaminated water of Japan’s Fukushima Daiichi nuclear power station, both of which also represent important propositions of China on promoting the three pillars of the NPT. During the Working Group meeting, we also submitted two working papers, one of which comprehensively elaborated on China’s views and proposals on strengthening the review process, and the other proposed important amendments on the Chair’s working paper (NPT/CONF.2026/PC.I /WP.34). It is our hope that these contributions from the Chinese delegation, together with other proposals put forward by all other parties, will constitute the basis for the discussions in future review process.

entries.position_summary: Reaffirms the NPT as the cornerstone of non-proliferation and calls for a productive review cycle, expressing readiness to work with all parties to achieve substantive outcomes for global peace, security, and development.
entries.quote: Mr. President, The NPT is the cornerstone of the international non-proliferation regime. In this era of tremendous changes, it’s even more crucial to promote this review cycle of the Treaty to achieve substantial outcome through the joint efforts of international community. CACDA stands ready to work with all parties towards this objective, making contributions to enhance peace, security and development of the world.

entries.position_summary: Supports continued, comprehensive discussions among all parties to enhance and strengthen the NPT review process.
entries.quote: manner. Third, all parties should endeavor to continue full and in-depth discussions of the views, propositions and suggestions on strengthening the review process.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates regular reporting on implementation of the 2010 Action Plan and Article VI and urges nuclear-weapon States to use standardized reporting formats and intervals, reinforcing the strengthened NPT review process.
entries.quote: 7. States parties have long reported to the Review Conference on their actions in support of the Non-Proliferation Treaty. As was stated in the consensus action plan from the 2010 Review Conference, “States parties should submit regular reports, within the framework of the strengthened review process for the Treaty, on the implementation of the present action plan, as well as of article VI”, along with disarmament actions recommended by the 1995 and 2000 Review Conferences (action 20). The action plan also encouraged the nuclear-weapon States to agree on a standard reporting form and to determine appropriate reporting intervals (action 21).

entries.position_summary: China, as incoming P5 coordinator, pledges to enhance P5 communication and cooperation to uphold the NPT, seeks productive outcomes from the current review cycle, and reaffirms support for the NPT’s universality, authority, and effectiveness.
entries.quote: China will soon take over as the P5 coordinator, and is willing to enhance the P5’s communication and cooperation on issues of upholding the NPT regime. We are also willing to work for fruitful outcomes of this round of the review process, and to jointly uphold the universality, authority and effectiveness of the NPT.

entries.position_summary: The United States backs a simplified, near-consensus proposal to enhance transparency by dedicating plenary time in PrepComs and the Review Conference to consider States parties’ national reports, especially those of nuclear-weapon States. It sees this procedural change as the best chance to secure consensus on transparency and accountability in the review cycle.
entries.quote: 10. The working group held extensive and constructive deliberations on proposals for transparency and accountability, focused on interactive discussion of national reports by States parties. Paragraphs 16 to 19 of the Chair’s working paper address this issue in detail, but fall short of consensus. A simplified alternative proposal submitted by four States parties came very close to achieving consensus, with only one State party objecting. The United States believes that this alternative, reproduced below, represents the best chance of gaining consensus in this area: The working group recommends that the Chairs of the Preparatory Committee meetings and the President of the Review Conference dedicate time in plenary sessions of the review cycle to consider the national reports of States parties, in particular the reports of the nuclear-weapon States, with regard to the 24-09063 3/5

entries.position_summary: Emphasizes implementing Article VI and commitments from past review conference outcomes. Calls for interactive consideration of reports and outcome-oriented PrepCom discussions to agree on recommendations that enhance the effectiveness, efficiency, coordination, continuity, transparency, and accountability of the NPT review process.
entries.quote: NPT/CONF.2026/PC.II/WP.4 implementation of article VI and commitments undertaken in final outcomes of review conferences. The consideration of the reports should provide for an interactive exchange. The working group recommends that the Preparatory Committee for the 2026 Review Conference should continue to engage in outcome-oriented discussions, with a view to agreeing on recommendations on improving the effectiveness, efficiency, coordination, continuity, transparency and accountability of the review process, on the basis of a balanced consideration of proposals presented during the working group.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates designating UNODA as the permanent secretariat for the NPT review process and formalizing a supporting role for the IAEA, particularly on non-proliferation and peaceful uses. This aims to institutionalize and streamline the review process and leverage IAEA expertise.
entries.quote: • Formalizing the United Nations role by establishing the Office for Disarmament Affairs as the permanent secretariat for the Non-Proliferation Treaty review process rather than deciding separately for each Non-Proliferation Treaty review cycle. Such a decision could also formalize the supporting role of the International Atomic Energy Agency in staffing the secretariat for issues of non-proliferation and the peaceful uses of nuclear technology.

entries.position_summary: The delegation highlights coordinating P5/N5 Track II dialogues focused on nuclear doctrine and nuclear risk reduction. It frames these efforts as contributions to promoting a successful NPT Review Conference.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, In the past year, CACDA had the honor to coordinate two P5/N5 Track II initiatives: the Expert-Level Track (ELT) and the Young Professionals Network (YPN) dialogue. Two on-line meetings and one in-person meeting were held respectively. The P5/N5 experts had candid and in-depth discussions on nuclear doctrine, nuclear risk reduction, and possible approaches to promote a successful NPT Review Conference.

entries.position_summary: China urges the P5 to fully implement NPT obligations and strengthen coordination to support and improve the NPT review process. It backs enhanced P5 nuclear policy exchanges and strategic risk-reduction dialogues, including on missile defense, outer space, cyberspace, and AI, to bolster transparency and strategic stability.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, China believes that the five nuclear-weapon States should work together to maintain the integrity and effectiveness of the NPT mechanism, and work together to fully implement all NPT obligations. For a long time, the P5 mechanism has played a unique and positive role in the NPT review process, and should continue to make its special contributions to such process. China has been actively promoting dialogue and cooperation among the P5 with practical actions, and advocates that the P5 further strengthen coordination and cooperation in promoting the NPT review process. China actively participates in nuclear policy exchanges among the P5 and dialogues on reducing strategic risks. On the basis of mutual respect and equality, China is willing to further the in-depth dialogue within the P5 mechanism, focusing on broad issues affecting strategic stability, such as missile defense systems, outer space, cyberspace, and artificial intelligence. 4

entries.position_summary: Supports building consensus in the PrepCom to lay a solid foundation for a successful NPT Review Conference next year.
entries.quote: To conclude, I wish this preparatory committee build further consensus, and lay a good foundation for achieving positive results at the review conference next year.

entries.position_summary: China urges NPT States parties to support its proposals and seeks in-depth P5 engagement, aiming for tangible outcomes at the 2026 Review Conference and preparatory meetings that can be incorporated into outcome documents.
entries.quote: 8. China calls on all States parties to the Treaty on the Non-proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (NPT) to positively respond to and support China’s propositions. China is ready to engage in in-depth discussions on this issue within the P5 framework. China looks forward to thorough discussions on the above propositions during the 2026 NPT Review Conference and its preparatory committee meetings, with a view to achieving tangible results that could be incorporated into the outcome documents. 2/2

entries.position_summary: The country proposes a procedural adjustment to streamline the Review Conference by assigning regional issues to either Main Committee I or II (but not both) and referring other treaty provisions to Main Committee III. This aims to reduce duplication and clarify committee mandates.
entries.quote: Regional issues could be remanded either to Main Committee I or II (but not both) and other provisions of the treaty to Main Committee III.

entries.position_summary: Supports presenting and discussing national reports in plenary sessions to ensure balanced treatment across all three NPT pillars and improve the review process.
entries.quote: • Since these national reports often cut across all three pillars, it would be an appropriate balance to present and discuss them in plenary sessions.

entries.position_summary: The statement highlights that the 1995 decision expanded the PrepCom’s mandate and time, including consideration of universality, but notes that efforts to produce substantive recommendations have consistently failed. It attributes this to the consensus rule and the practice that nothing is agreed until everything is agreed, which would require pre-negotiating compromises a year before the Review Conference.
entries.quote: 11. Until the 1995 Review and Extension Conference, Preparatory Committee meetings focused on administrative matters and making the necessary decisions and arrangements for the Review Conference. The 1995 decision on strengthening the review process gave the Preparatory Committee more time (three meetings vs one) and a much broader mandate “to consider principles, objectives and ways in order to promote the full implementation of the Treaty, as well as its universality, and to make recommendations thereon to the Review Conference”. Since then, the Chairs of the Preparatory Committee, particularly at its third session, have sought to build consensus on substantive recommendations to the Review Conference. However, this ambition has never been achieved. Reaching consensus at review conferences has proven to be difficult enough, often hinging on key compromises sought during the final week of the Conference. And since many States parties adopt the approach that “nothing is agreed until everything is agreed”, making substantive recommendations would essentially require pre-negotiating such compromises a full year in advance.

entries.position_summary: Calls for continued, in-depth discussions to strengthen the NPT review process, noting the failure to agree reflects the complexity of challenges across the Treaty’s three pillars. Affirms the NPT’s irreplaceable status and urges all States Parties to uphold its object and purpose while strengthening its authority, effectiveness, and universality.
entries.quote: Through the Working Group’s work in the past five days, I believe that all parties have understood better that various positions and proposals on strengthening the treaty review process need to be explored further in future’s review process. That the parties failed to reach an agreement is not only the result of different perspectives in observing and understanding international situation, differences in the positions and ideas, still less political will. More importantly, many challenges facing the NPT’s three pillars are in themselves diverse, complex, and unprecedented. Progress in strengthening the review process will only be possible on the premise of continuing in-depth discussions on all relevant issues. Standing at the new starting point of the NPT review process, States parties should agree on at least the following three points: First, the status and role of the NPT regime is irreplaceable. All States parties should work together to uphold and strengthen the authority, effectiveness and universality of the NPT. Second, the object and purpose of the NPT must be upheld.

entries.position_summary: China urges a balanced focus on disarmament, non-proliferation, and peaceful uses to address urgent arms control challenges and produce substantive results in the current review cycle. It emphasizes upholding the NPT’s authority and effectiveness and views the success of this review cycle as crucial.
entries.quote: NPT /CONF.2026/PC.II/WP.35 Preparatory Committee for the 2026 Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the 12 July 2024 Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons Original: Chinese ADVANCE UNEDITED VERSION Second session Geneva, 22 July–2 August 2024 Nuclear arms control Working paper submitted by China Today, the global security environment has experienced profound and complex changes. The international arms control regime is faced with serious challenges. Since 2010, two consecutive NPT review cycles ended without a consensus outcome. Thus the success of the current review cycle is of great importance. China believes that all sides should focus on urgent issues of the nuclear arms control area, promote nuclear disarmament, nuclear non-proliferation, and peaceful uses of nuclear energy in a comprehensive and balanced manner on the basis of mutual respect, ensure that this review cycle could produce substantive results and to jointly uphold the authority and effectiveness of the NPT.

entries.position_summary: China advocates a cooperative, indivisible security approach and commits to dialogue to advance the NPT review process. It supports strengthening the NPT’s universality, authority, and effectiveness.
entries.quote: Last year, Chinese President Xi Jinping proposed a Global Security Initiative at the opening ceremony of the Boao Forum. He stressed the importance of upholding a vision of common, comprehensive, cooperative and sustainable security.An enduring solution to global security challenges lies in upholding the principle of indivisible security, taking each other’s legitimate security concerns seriously, and building a balanced, effective and sustainable security architecture with a view to universal and common security.The ChinaArms Control and DisarmamentAssociation is willing to continue to strengthen dialogue and cooperation with all parties, push for progress in the new round of the NPT review process, continuously strengthen the universality, authority and effectiveness of the NPT, and make new contributions to promoting peace, security anddevelopment oftheworld.

entries.position_summary: The country urges strengthening multilateral arms control treaties and mechanisms and rejects unilateral or bloc-based 'house rules' supplanting the UN Charter and existing regimes. It regrets the 10th NPT RevCon’s failure to reach consensus but calls on all States Parties to fully implement NPT obligations, safeguard the three pillars, and enhance the Treaty’s role.
entries.quote: Secondly, we must earnestly abide by and strengthen international arms control treaties and mechanisms. The "house rules" established by a certain country or bloc of countries must not replace the UN Charter and the current existing international arms control treaties and mechanisms. It is regrettable that the 10th NPT Review Conference did not reach consensus on an outcome document, however theendeavour to uphold the objective and purpose of the NPT should never end. All the State parties should continue to faithfully fulfill all the obligations under the NPT, firmly safeguard the three pillars of the Treaty, and promote a greater role of NPT in serving peace, development andstability.

entries.position_summary: The country underscores the NPT as the cornerstone of the regime, calling for balanced advancement of disarmament, non-proliferation, and peaceful uses, and for a successful start to the 11th NPT review cycle while upholding multilateral arms control forums. It opposes geopolitical ‘small blocs’ and nuclear cooperation it views as contrary to the NPT’s object and purpose—implicitly criticizing arrangements such as nuclear-powered submarine cooperation—while supporting peaceful nuclear uses under the Treaty.
entries.quote: Fourthly, maintain and strengthen the international nuclear disarmament and nuclear non-proliferation regime with NPT as its cornerstone. The international community should advance the three pillars of nuclear disarmament, nuclear non-proliferation, and peaceful uses of nuclear energy in a balanced manner, oppose double standards and pragmatism, firmly safeguard the effectiveness and authority of the treaty, and promote the successful start of the eleventh round of the NPT review process. We should resist all kinds of small blocs and small circles, maintain multilateral arms control mechanisms such as the First Committee of the UN General Assembly and the Conference on Disarmament, and give full play to their roles. We should resolutely oppose relevant countries’ cooperation in the nuclear field that runs counter to the object and purpose of the NPT, which are based on narrow geopolitical considerations.

entries.position_summary: The country advocates strengthening the non-proliferation regime with the NPT at its core and urges leveraging the current PrepCom session to address proliferation challenges and build broad consensus.
entries.quote: Under the current circumstances, strengthening the international nuclear non-proliferation regime with NPT as its cornerstone and diminishing the risks of nuclear-weapon proliferation is of great significance to improving global nuclear governance and promoting global peace and security. All parties should seize the opportunity of the third session of preparatory committee of this review cycle to explore solutions to the challenges of nuclear proliferation, form broad consensus

entries.position_summary: The country advocates streamlining the NPT review process by reducing duplication and enhancing its effectiveness and efficiency. It supports considering options to improve how the review is organized and conducted.
entries.quote: 5. Several options could be considered for reducing duplication and improving effectiveness and efficiency in the review process:

entries.position_summary: The country supports improving the NPT review process by carefully organizing sessions, curating the mix of national reports, structuring and moderating discussions, and facilitating Q&A. It favors submitting written questions in advance to enhance efficiency and accountability.
entries.quote: • The organization of each session, including the number and mix of national reports, the structure and moderation of the discussion and answering questions from other States parties, should be considered carefully. Given the time constraints, providing questions in advance in writing could help to structure the discussion.

entries.position_summary: It underscores that, per Article VIII(3), the NPT review process is meant to assess the Treaty's operation to ensure its purposes and provisions are realized, and recalls the 1995 decision to strengthen the review process. The emphasis is on reinforcing and adhering to the agreed framework for a stronger review mechanism.
entries.quote: 3. The purpose of the Non-Proliferation Treaty review process, as set out in article VIII, paragraph 3, of the Treaty, is “to review the operation of this Treaty with a view to assuring that the purposes of the Preamble and the provisions of the Treaty are being realized”. In its decision on strengthening the review process, the 1995 Non- __________________ 1 Available at https://docs-library.unoda.org/Treaty_on_the_Non-Proliferation_of_Nuclear_ Weapons_-Working_group_on_further_strengthening_the_review_process_(2023)/09._WG- _USA.pdf. 24-09063 (E) 280524 *2409063*

entries.position_summary: China advocates that the 2026 NPT Review Conference and its Preparatory Sessions hold in-depth discussions and aim to forge consensus language for inclusion in the Final Document, emphasizing a focused and outcome-oriented review process.
entries.quote: China suggests that the 2026 NPT Review Conference and its Preparatory Sessions discuss the following issues in-depth and strive for these consensus to be included in the Final Document.

entries.position_summary: China underscores the NPT as the cornerstone of the regime and commits to constructive engagement in the second PrepCom. It calls for balanced progress across disarmament, non-proliferation, and peaceful uses, emphasizing the NPT’s role in security and development.
entries.quote: The treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (NPT) is the cornerstone of the international nuclear non-proliferation regime, and an important pillar of the global security and development governance system. China is willing to work with international community to constructively participate in the second session of the preparatory committee, advance the three pillars of nuclear disarmament, nuclear non-proliferation and the peaceful uses of nuclear energy in a balanced manner, and give full play to the role of the NPT in serving security and development in the new era.

entries.position_summary: China reaffirms its commitment to fully implement the NPT and pledges to work with all Parties, including the five nuclear-weapon states, to strengthen the Treaty’s review process.
entries.quote: Mr. Chair, I would like to emphasize that China firmly upholds the NPT and has never shied away from its obligations to fully implement the Treaty. We will continue to actively participate in and work with all NPT members, including the five nuclear- weapon States, to strengthen the review process of the treaty in a highly responsible attitude towards international peace and security, the international nuclear non- 2

entries.position_summary: The statement criticizes great-power competition and bloc confrontation for heightening arms race and conflict risks, straining the NPT-based nonproliferation regime. It calls for using the new NPT review process to strengthen dialogue and cooperation and uphold the NPT’s authority and effectiveness.
entries.quote: In recent years, the world has witnessed a persistent and unchecked pandemic, accelerating changes unseen in a century, and a period of turbulence and transformation. Some countries are obsessed with the so-called "great power competition strategy" and focus on the "Cold War mentality" of bloc confrontation, leading to the deterioration of the global strategic security environment ， the aggravation of the risk of arms race and military conflicts, which has seriously affected global security and stability. As a result, the international non-proliferation regime with the NPT as its cornerstone is significantly strained and even faces new and severe challenges since the end ofthe ColdWar. Under thecurrent circumstances, the international community must make full use of the new NPT review process, strengthen dialogue and cooperation, jointly uphold the authority and effectiveness of theNPT,and promotethebuilding ofacommunity witha shared future formankind.

entries.position_summary: The country urges full and effective implementation of past NPT Review Conference outcomes, including the 1995 decisions and Middle East resolution, the 2000 final document commitments, and the 2010 action plan. This is framed as necessary to strengthen the review process and advance agreed disarmament commitments.
entries.quote: Fully and effectively implement the past outcomes and commitments of the NPT review process, including the decisions and resolution of the 1995 Review and Extension Conference, the commitments contained in the 2000 Review Conference final document and the action plan of the 2010 Review Conference.
